annotations.label: Presentation of a group

anchor.snippet: a presentation is one method of specifying a group

annotations.note: `PresentedGroup rels` builds the group on generators `α` with relations `rels`, exactly specifying a group by generators and relations.

annotations.label: Formal definition (quotient of free group)

anchor.snippet: the group G is said to have the above presentation if it is isomorphic to the quotient

annotations.note: `PresentedGroup` is defined as `FreeGroup α ⧸ Subgroup.normalClosure rels`, the quotient-of-free-group construction described here.

annotations.label: Cyclic group of order n

anchor.snippet: the cyclic group of order n has the presentation

annotations.note: Cyclic groups exist in Mathlib (e.g. `ZMod n`), but no declaration identifies a cyclic group with the presentation ⟨a | aⁿ⟩.

annotations.label: Relators

anchor.snippet: Such terms are called relators

annotations.note: Relators are captured as the parameter `rels : Set (FreeGroup α)` of `PresentedGroup`, but there is no standalone named definition of a relator.

annotations.label: Free group on a set

anchor.snippet: A free group on a set S is a group where each element can be uniquely described

annotations.note: `FreeGroup α` is the free group on a type `α` (with `IsFreeGroup`/`FreeGroupBasis` capturing the universal property).

annotations.label: Dihedral group D8 presentation

anchor.snippet: the dihedral group D 8 of order sixteen can be generated by a rotation

annotations.note: `DihedralGroup n` is formalized directly via elements `r i`/`sr i`, not as a `PresentedGroup`, so the presentation form of D8 is not formalized.

annotations.label: Group defined by a presentation

anchor.snippet: Let S be a set and let F S be the free group on S

annotations.note: `PresentedGroup rels = FreeGroup α ⧸ Subgroup.normalClosure rels` is precisely the free group on `S` quotiented by the normal closure of `R`.

annotations.label: Group has a presentation

anchor.snippet: A group G is said to have the presentation

annotations.note: The construction `PresentedGroup` exists but there is no named predicate for an abstract `G` being isomorphic to a given presentation.

annotations.label: Presentation from multiplication table

anchor.snippet: it is possible to build a presentation of G from the group multiplication table

annotations.note: No declaration constructs a presentation of a group from its Cayley/multiplication table.

annotations.label: Alternate definition via equivalence classes

anchor.snippet: The definition of group presentation may alternatively be recast in terms of equivalence classes of words

annotations.note: `FreeGroup` is itself defined as a quotient (equivalence classes) of words and `PresentedGroup` quotients further, but this equivalence-class recasting is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Finitely generated, related, and presented

anchor.snippet: A presentation is said to be finitely generated if S is finite

annotations.note: `Group.FG` formalizes finitely generated and `Group.IsFinitelyPresented` formalizes finitely presented (finite generators with finitely-generated normal closure of relations).

annotations.label: One-relator group

anchor.snippet: is called a one-relator group

annotations.note: No definition of a one-relator group (presentation with a single relator) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursive presentation

anchor.snippet: the presentation is a recursive presentation and the corresponding group is recursively presented

annotations.note: Recursive/computable presentations of groups are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursively enumerable relators imply recursive presentation

anchor.snippet: if a group has a presentation with R recursively enumerable then it has another one with R recursive

annotations.note: This Craig's-trick result on recursively enumerable versus recursive relator sets is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finitely presented implies recursively presented

anchor.snippet: Every finitely presented group is recursively presented

annotations.note: Recursive presentation is not defined in Mathlib, so this implication is not formalized.

annotations.label: Higman embedding theorem

anchor.snippet: a finitely generated group has a recursive presentation if and only if it can be embedded in a finitely presented group

annotations.note: The Higman embedding theorem is not formalized (only HNN extensions, used in its proof, exist in `Mathlib.GroupTheory.HNNExtension`).

annotations.label: Countably many finitely generated recursively presented groups

anchor.snippet: there are (up to isomorphism) only countably many finitely generated recursively presented groups

annotations.note: This countability result for recursively presented groups is not formalized.

annotations.label: Neumann: uncountably many two-generator groups

anchor.snippet: there are uncountably many non-isomorphic two generator groups

annotations.note: B.H. Neumann's theorem on uncountably many two-generator groups is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finitely generated groups not recursively presented

anchor.snippet: there are finitely generated groups that cannot be recursively presented

annotations.note: The existence of finitely generated groups with no recursive presentation is not formalized.

annotations.label: Wreath product not finitely presented

anchor.snippet: An example of a finitely generated group that is not finitely presented is the wreath product

annotations.note: A wreath product construction (`RegularWreathProduct`) exists, but the claim that ℤ≀ℤ is not finitely presented is not formalized.

annotations.label: Every group has a presentation

anchor.snippet: Every group has a presentation

annotations.note: The `PresentedGroup` quotient construction and surjections from free groups exist, but there is no theorem stating every group is isomorphic to some `PresentedGroup`.

annotations.label: Every finite group has a finite presentation

anchor.snippet: Every finite group has a finite presentation

annotations.note: The predicate `Group.IsFinitelyPresented` exists but there is no instance/theorem establishing it for all finite groups.

annotations.label: Novikov–Boone theorem

anchor.snippet: there is no algorithm which, given two words u , v , decides whether u and v describe the same element in the group

annotations.note: The undecidability of the word problem (Novikov–Boone) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Free product presentation

anchor.snippet: the free product G ∗ H has presentation

annotations.note: The free product `Monoid.Coprod` (with its universal property) is formalized, but the statement combining presentations of the factors is not.

annotations.label: Direct product presentation

anchor.snippet: the direct product G × H has presentation

annotations.note: The direct product `G × H` as a group is formalized, but the presentation (union of generators/relations plus commutators) is not.

annotations.label: Semidirect product presentation

anchor.snippet: the semidirect product G ⋊ φ H has presentation

annotations.note: The `SemidirectProduct` is formalized, but the statement combining presentations of the factors into the semidirect product presentation is not.

annotations.label: Deficiency

anchor.snippet: The deficiency of a finite presentation

annotations.note: The deficiency of a finite presentation/group is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Deficiency of a finite group is non-positive

anchor.snippet: The deficiency of a finite group is non-positive

annotations.note: Deficiency is not formalized, so this non-positivity result is not present.

annotations.label: Schur multiplicator bound and efficient groups

anchor.snippet: The Schur multiplicator of a finite group G can be generated by

annotations.note: The Schur multiplier and the notion of efficient groups are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Word metric and Cayley graph geometry

anchor.snippet: A presentation of a group determines a geometry

annotations.note: The Cayley graph `SimpleGraph.mulCayley` is formalized, but the associated word metric and resulting geometry are not.

annotations.label: Coarse geometry is intrinsic

anchor.snippet: some properties of this graph (the coarse geometry ) are intrinsic

annotations.note: Quasi-isometry invariance of the coarse geometry of a Cayley graph is not formalized.
